# Codex Multi-Agent Runtime Contract

This file defines the project-side contract for the currently available Codex
collaboration runtime. `.claude/**` remains the source of truth. Generated
`.agents/**` and `.codex/**` files do not override this contract.

## Ownership

The root parent owns all shared orchestration state: plans, waves, slot
allocation, checkpoints, manifests, aggregation, retries, conflict resolution,
and resume decisions. Child agents return evidence; they do not advance shared
state or spawn nested work.

There is no Claude Agent Team runtime in Codex. Do not assume a shared team,
shared inbox, unread-message counter, shared task list, raw child output stream,
background scheduler, callback, or autonomous recurring loop.

## Approval, authority, and native permission

Lifecycle/product approval, owner authority for risky or destructive effects,
and native Codex sandbox/elevation permission are separate decisions. Durable
lifecycle approval authorizes a product transition; owner authority authorizes
the intended risky effect; neither grants sandbox access. The agent needing
elevated execution must issue the native Codex tool request with the exact
command and justification. Chat approval is never a substitute for that native
request, and agents must never ask the user to type a magic authorization phrase.

`awaiting_user` is only for a conversational decision, not a pending native
permission card. Asking such a question permits one atomic checkpoint update,
then a quiescent wait: automatic or goal-continuation turns must not prepare
extra work, rewrite the checkpoint, or repeat the request without new user or
external evidence. A denied or failed sandbox request is retried only through
another native tool request when still necessary and safe. It must stop and
return control first: a new native request requires new user instruction or a
materially changed exact command/justification; automatic or goal-continuation
alone must never reissue permission cards. No chat-phrase loop.

Gate-producing privileged commands that combine elevation with temporary files,
ACLs, parsing, cleanup, or multiple subprocesses must use a checked-in, tested
runner or simple exact argv calls. Ad-hoc multiline or inline PowerShell is
forbidden. Validate the command against the actual shell/runtime before the
privileged run; preserve fail-closed behavior and cleanup evidence.

## Native collaboration primitives

### `spawn_agent({task_name, message, fork_turns, agent_type?, model?, reasoning_effort?})`

Starts an asynchronous child and returns its agent id/canonical task name.

- `task_name` is a routing label, not proof that a role prompt or model profile
  was bound.
- `message` is the explicit task contract delivered to the child.
- `fork_turns` accepts `"none"`, `"all"`, or a positive recent-turn count as a
  string. Project policy is `default_fork_turns = "none"`: callers must pass
  `"none"` explicitly for normal tasks. The platform may otherwise default an
  omitted value to broader history.
- Use a positive count or `"all"` only when the task genuinely requires that
  conversation context and the parent has bounded the exposure.
- Runtime-managed/fixed roles use `agent_type="<exact-role>"` and omit `model`
  and `reasoning_effort`. The active tool schema is the authority for
  whether a named role exists.
- A generic/custom/default worker uses `agent_type="default"` only when the
  active schema exposes that capability. It may pass `model` and
  `reasoning_effort` only when the active schema supports those fields and
  `fork_turns` is `"none"` or a bounded recent-turn count.
- A successful spawn proves selector acceptance, not an independent
  resolved-model trace. Record the requested selector and acceptance or failure.
  Record a resolved trace only if exposed by the runtime.

### `followup_task(target, message)`

Delivers follow-up work and triggers another turn when the target is idle. If
the target is running, delivery occurs at a safe message boundary or after its
pending tool call. Use it for another work turn on the same agent, not as an
implicit shared inbox protocol.

### `send_message(target, message)`

Queues a message for an existing agent but does not trigger a new turn. Use it
only when a running agent will naturally reach a message boundary. A queued
message is not evidence that it was read or acted upon.

### `wait_agent(timeout_ms)`

Waits for a mailbox/status update from any live agent. It is a bounded wait
primitive, not a background scheduler. It may return because of agent activity,
timeout, or newly steered user input. The parent must re-evaluate state after
every return.

### `list_agents(path_prefix?)`

Returns the live agent tree and coarse runtime status. It does not expose
unread counts, full inboxes, raw output, or semantic progress. `running` means
only that a turn is active; **running is not progress**.

### `interrupt_agent(target)`

Interrupts the target's current turn and returns its previous status. The agent
remains addressable for later follow-up. Interrupted threads may retain runtime
capacity until the platform reports otherwise; never assume an interrupt
immediately frees a slot.

## Capacity and depth

The source policy accepts a configured `max_threads` in the sane range `1..6`;
this project's configured cap is five. The shared scheduling formula is:
`confirmed free children = min(configured cap - current active agents
(including root), live runtime reported free child slots, explicitly named
workload-specific cap)`. The workload-specific term may constrain a named wave,
but it never invents capacity. Effective total capacity remains
`min(configured cap, live runtime availability)`, including the root parent.
Every worker, reviewer, observer, or watchdog counts against the configured cap.

Project maximum depth is one: only the root parent may spawn. Children must not
fan out. Before allocation, the parent checks the live agent inventory and the
current runtime's advertised availability rather than assuming the configured
cap is available. If capacity is ambiguous after interruption or completion,
apply the bounded fail-closed escape below instead of waiting indefinitely.
For capacity ambiguity specifically, the parent bounds its availability
re-checks, then schedules with confirmed-free slots (the min() formula)
rather than parking or escalating — capacity ambiguity is not an
environment blocker.

## Bounded fail-closed escape and blocker classification

On any blocker, an agent gets a bounded self-diagnosis budget: at most two
attempts. One attempt is one hypothesis or reproduction command run to a
definite pass or fail result, including its immediate follow-up reads. If
the second attempt does not resolve the blocker, the agent stops, fails
closed, and returns its result citing the unmet precondition. A fresh
running status never extends the budget.

Blockers fall into two classes. CODE-FIXABLE means a repo file edit can
resolve it: a bug, a missing test, a wrong import, a logic error.
ENVIRONMENT or DEFERRED-EVIDENCE means no repo edit can resolve it: a
missing engine, image, credential, network path, or live or production
access. A repo edit that only masks the blocker, for example skipping a
test because its engine is missing, does not make the blocker CODE-FIXABLE.

The first fail-closed confirmation of an environment or deferred-evidence
blocker — the first agent result that cites the unmet precondition — is
escalated by the parent to the owner within the same round (the current
review or fix cycle for that task, before any further worker spawn): a
concrete owner question naming the blocker, what was tried across the
bounded diagnosis attempts, and the decision needed. The parent must not
route an environment blocker into another coder fix-round; no repo edit
changes an unmet environment precondition. A code-fixable blocker continues
through the ordinary review or fix cycle instead.

The owner question follows the awaiting_user rules above: one atomic
checkpoint update, then a quiescent wait. Example: Blocker: local Postgres
engine missing. Tried: direct pytest run; service lookup on the machine.
Decision needed: provide the engine, or accept the declared deferral and
proceed without the integration run.

## Reviewer deferred-evidence calibration

When a task declares an evidence item deferred or opt-in — for example an
integration test gated behind an opt-in flag because the local environment
lacks a required engine — that declared deferral is not a Critical or
blocking review finding. Reviewers evaluate code and test design against
the task-declared evidence model, not against evidence the task itself
marked unavailable. A sound, referenced declared deferral is never a
Critical or blocking finding in any round, and once the parent upholds it
the reasoning question is closed.

A valid deferral must name where and when the deferred evidence will be
produced (e.g. the opt-in flag and the CI/live environment that runs it);
evidence for a mandatory gate (security, QA, terminal) is never deferrable.
A deferral the reviewer assesses as unsound, or one lacking a produce-later
reference, is a blocking finding, not an exempt deferral.

The parent enforces this when aggregating reviewer findings: a finding that
only restates an already declared deferred-evidence item does not consume
another fix round.

## Progress and heartbeat

Runtime status is not a progress oracle. For work expected to outlive one
natural wait boundary, the parent records an artifact/test heartbeat in the
feature checkpoint or canonical run log:

```yaml
agent_id: <runtime id>
task_id: <task id>
observed_at: <ISO-8601 UTC>
artifact_paths: []
artifact_fingerprints: {}
verification:
  command: null
  result: not_run
last_completed_step: null
blocker: null
```

A heartbeat is meaningful only when at least one durable artifact fingerprint,
completed step, or test result changed. Repeated timestamps, chat assurances,
and `running` status do not count. Parent supervision occurs at natural
wait/yield boundaries: child completion, a delivered result, a bounded
`wait_agent` return, an explicit deadline, user input, or session recovery.

## Role instructions and model preferences

`.claude/codex/agent-profiles.toml` maps each semantic role to its canonical
`.claude/agents/<name>.md` source and records the desired model/effort policy.
Role binding and model selection are separate capabilities:

- A Codex child is role-bound when the parent validates the profile-to-source
  mapping, explicitly instructs the isolated child to read and follow that exact
  role source, and supplies a bounded task envelope with scope, allowed writes,
  required evidence, review/test gates, ownership, and deadline. Selector and
  role binding for ordinary single-turn work are established by the validated
  mapping, bounded envelope, and native spawn result. For privileged/high-risk
  work they are recorded in the parent-owned receipt described below.
- `task_name` remains a routing label. It is never sufficient on its own, and a
  child self-report or TOML existence does not replace the role-source and task-
  envelope checks.
- `model` and `effort` are desired preferences in the profile registry.
  Generic/custom selectable workers may pass a requested model and reasoning
  effort when the active schema supports them. Record the requested selector
  and its acceptance or failure. A successful spawn proves selector acceptance,
  not an independent resolved-model trace; record a resolved trace only if
  exposed. When selectors are unavailable, record model binding as
  `unsupported` or `unverified`, do not claim a model override, and continue
  with the role-bound child.

### Ordinary single-turn execution

An ordinary low-risk `coder` and an ordinary read-only reviewer use a single native spawn
with a bounded envelope. They require no authorization handshake
and no filesystem receipt. The parent still validates the canonical role
source, owns scope and aggregation, and accepts work only with the requested
diff or report plus verification evidence. This also covers local,
read-only, non-production evidence gathering, for example checking whether a
required engine, image, or tool is present on the machine.

Two-turn binding and native permission safeguards remain for any privileged
or high-risk effect as defined by the Privileged-effect boundary in
`.codex/privileged-roles.md`; that section owns the canonical trigger list
and the receipt and authorization protocol.

## Agent memory

Claude Code may honor source frontmatter such as `memory: project` through its
native behavior. Codex has no automatic shared or cross-session agent memory.
When prior lessons are present and relevant, the parent task envelope explicitly
instructs the Codex role-bound child to read the canonical
`.claude/agent-memory/{agent}/MEMORY.md` as read-only input. The child must not
write it, treat it as hidden persistence, or infer that a later worker/thread
will receive it automatically.

## Recovery

Agent identity and chat history are not durable recovery state. On compaction,
restart, rate limit, or interruption, the parent reads canonical artifacts and
the last heartbeat, then decides whether to use `followup_task`, spawn a fresh
child with `fork_turns: "none"`, or stop for user input. No child, watchdog, or
timer resumes a wave autonomously.
